coredata.dc:title: How a mixture of microRNA-29a (miR-29a) and microRNA-144 (miR-144) cancer biomarkers interacts with a graphene quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Traiphothon, Darunee
Awang, Tadsanee
Kuntip, Nattapon
Japrung, Deanpen
Pongprayoon, Prapasiri
coredata.dc:description: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) which are small non-coding RNAs have been reported to be potential cancer biomarker. However, it is difficult to extract such short RNA from a sample matrix. New effective strategies are required. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used to detect nucleotides in many biosensor platforms, but their applications for miRNA extraction remain limited. GQD was reported to be able to collect short miRNA, but its performance to collect miRNAs with different structure remains unknown. Thus, in this work, the capability of GQD to interact with two different miRNAs is investigated. A mixture of hairpin-like miR-29a and circular miR-144 molecules are used as a representative of two miRNA morphologies. Two systems (a miRNA mixture, comprising 4 of miR-29a and 4 of miR-144, with (miR_GQD) and without GQD (miR)) were studied in comparison. MiRNAs in a mixture (miR) can aggregate, but no permanent miRNA assembly is captured. In contrast, the presence of GQD can rapidly and spontaneously activate the permanent miRNA/GQD clustering. This finding highlights the ability of GQD to be a miRNA collector. Interestingly, all GQD-bound miRNAs do not unfold. This allows the easy accessibility for probes. Also, nano-sized GQD seems to prefer hairpin miR-29a. The free 5’ terminus of miR-29a acts as the sticky end to adhere on GQD. This work highlights the importance of RNA secondary structure on GQD/miRNA aggregation capability. An insight obtained here will be useful for further design of miRNA isolation strategies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: miRNA
miR-29a
miR-144
Graphene quantum dot
Cancer biomarker
MD simulations

P2351465 and P2250764) and the Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council, the Office of the Prime Minister through Kasetsart University under the project entitled \"Driving Research and Development of Cutting-edge Innovations for ASEAN's Agricultural Leadership\" for financial support for financial supports. We are also thankful for the computer facilities from Department of Computer Engineering, Kasetsart University and NSTDA supercomputer center (ThaiSC).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cartoon views of miR-29a (A) and miR-144 (B) with sequences. (C) and (D) display two systems (miR and miR_GQD) studied in this work.
Root-mean-square deviations (RMSDs) of each miRNA in miR (A) and miR_GQD (B) where their orientations are shown on the right. (C) Snapshots of miR (top) and miR_GQD (bottom) as a function of time.
(A) Number of self-hydrogen bonds of each miR chain with standard deviation in both miR and miR_GQD systems. (B) Number of miR contacts with water and GQD in miR and miR_GQD.

Number of hydrogen bonds between two chains in miR (A) and miR_GQD (B) systems where the final snapshot is shown on the right. All chains are labelled in different colours. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Frequencies of GQD-miRNA contacts of miR-29a (A) and miR-144 (B) where structures of both miRNAs are shown on the right. (C) Cartoon views of four GQD-bound miRNA molecules (chains A, B, and D are miR-29a and chain F is miR-144) where key residues for adhesion are also labelled.
Number of self-hydrogen bonds of each chain in all systems with standard deviation.
Average number of miR-GQD and miR-water contacts with standard deviation in all systems.

head.ce:title: How a mixture of microRNA-29a (miR-29a) and microRNA-144 (miR-144) cancer biomarkers interacts with a graphene quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Nanotechnology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Thailand Science Park, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Nanotechnology Center, National Science and Technology Development Agency, Thailand Science Park, Pathumthani, 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical, Food, and Agricultural Industries, KU Institute for Advanced Studies, Kasetsart University, Bangkok, 10900 Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical, Food, and Agricultural Industries

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Advanced Studied in Nanotechnology for Chemical, Food, and Agricultural Industries, KU Institute for Advanced Studies, Kasetsart University, Bangkok, 10900 Thailand

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Kasetsart University, Chatuchak, Bangkok, 10900 Thailand.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) which are small non-coding RNAs have been reported to be potential cancer biomarker. However, it is difficult to extract such short RNA from a sample matrix. New effective strategies are required. Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used to detect nucleotides in many biosensor platforms, but their applications for miRNA extraction remain limited. GQD was reported to be able to collect short miRNA, but its performance to collect miRNAs with different structure remains unknown. Thus, in this work, the capability of GQD to interact with two different miRNAs is investigated. A mixture of hairpin-like miR-29a and circular miR-144 molecules are used as a representative of two miRNA morphologies. Two systems (a miRNA mixture, comprising 4 of miR-29a and 4 of miR-144, with (miR_GQD) and without GQD (miR)) were studied in comparison. MiRNAs in a mixture (miR) can aggregate, but no permanent miRNA assembly is captured. In contrast, the presence of GQD can rapidly and spontaneously activate the permanent miRNA/GQD clustering. This finding highlights the ability of GQD to be a miRNA collector. Interestingly, all GQD-bound miRNAs do not unfold. This allows the easy accessibility for probes. Also, nano-sized GQD seems to prefer hairpin miR-29a. The free 5’ terminus of miR-29a acts as the sticky end to adhere on GQD. This work highlights the importance of RNA secondary structure on GQD/miRNA aggregation capability. An insight obtained here will be useful for further design of miRNA isolation strategies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNA (miRNA) is a class of small non-coding RNAs (approximately 19–22 nucleotides in length) that play a crucial role in post-transcriptional gene regulation in eukaryotes [1]. In the circulation, miRNAs exist in very stable forms [2]. Aberrant miRNA expression in tissues or body fluids has been documented in multiple diseases including cardiovascular diseases, autoimmune diseases, tuberculosis, and cancers [3,4]. Furthermore, the pathology specificity of circulating miRNAs makes them become an excellent candidate for next generation of cancer biomarkers [5]. Many studies have been made to design and develop novel techniques to extract and detect miRNAs from a biological sample [6,7]. Effective extraction and purification of miRNA from biological samples are crucial to enhance the sensitivity of miRNA detection [8]. Many errors during the miRNA detection can be occurred due to poorly controlled sample preparation and extraction. The existing methods for miRNA extractions often involve the use for organic solvents or solid sorbent-packed columns, but these current techniques provide a low recovery for short nucleotides [9] which are incompatible for miRNAs. Therefore, new methods for capturing small miRNA are demanded. Various nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes (CNs), and metal nanoparticles (MNs) have been used to collect DNA/RNA from a sample solution [9–14]. The adsorption/desorption process of RNA/DNA onto nanomaterials is one of keys for achieving efficient DNA/RNA isolation. However, the current nanomaterial-based techniques are primarily designed for long single-stranded DNA/RNA molecules [15,16] where the applications of nanomaterial-based miRNA extraction strategies remained limited. The efficiency of miRNA detection depends on the extraction method [17,18], therefore the new isolation methods with high quality and quantity of miRNAs recovered are needed.

A graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a zero-dimensional material comprising one or more graphene sheets with sizes ranging from 3 to 20 nm [19–21]. GQDs are one of promising nanomaterials for biological applications due to their high biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity [22–24]. GQDs also act as an ideal substrate for biomolecules and quenching agent at the same time [25], thus GQDs have been used in many biosensor platforms [23,26,27]. Although graphene oxide was reported to display a good performance in extracting short RNAs from biological samples [28–30], the more biocompatibility and lower toxicity of GQDs are attractive enough to study their performances for miRNA isolation. Recently, the computational studies have reported the promising potential of GQDs to collect miRNAs in a solution [8,31,32]. However, only one type of miRNAs (miR-29a) was studied there, while the ability of GQDs to collect a miRNA mixture remains unknown.

Thus, in this work, the possibility of using GQD to collect a mixture of miRNAs are investigated. The aggregation of two miRNAs (miR-29a and miR-144) on a GQD is studied. A small graphene (GRA) sheet with a dimension of 4.2 × 4.9 nm2 is employed to represent a GQD model. MiR-29a and miR-144 are used as a model of miRNA mixture. Both miRNAs have been recommended to be one of key cancer biomarkers due to its high expression in the blood circulation in cancer patients [33,34]. MiR-29a and miR-144 have different morphologies where miR-29a is hairpin-like whereas miR-144 has a circular-like structure where its 5′ and 3′- ends non-covalently pair up. Their structure can be seen in Fig. 1 . To explore the aggregation of miRNA mixture and GQD in a microscopic level, Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations were performed here. MD simulations have been successfully conducted to explore the aggregation mechanisms of many nucleic acids onto graphene and its derivatives [13,35,36]. Herein, the assembly mechanism of miRNA mixture and GQD are studied. Two systems containing four miR-29a and four miR-144 molecules with and without GQD, namely miR_GQD and miR were studied as seen in Fig. 1C and D. The GQD-free miRNA system (miR) was studied in parallel in order to understand the effect of GQD on miRNA clustering. An insight obtained here can be beneficial to improve and devise the miRNA extraction approaches in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of miR-29a/miR-144 mixture and miR-29a/miR-144 mixture in complex with GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The starting 3D structure of miR-29a (5′-UAG CAC CAU CUG AAA UCG GUU A-3′) was obtained from a previous work [8], while the structure of miR-144 (5′-GGA UAU CAU CAU AUA CUG UAA G-3′) were constructed using the RNAcomposer server [37]. The folded structure of miR-144 was modelled by placing miR-144 in a cubic box (9 x 9 × 9 nm3) with TIP3P water molecules and counterions. A miR-144 system was energy minimised for 50,00 steps with steepest descent algorithm. The 10-ns equilibration run was then performed and followed by 1-μs production run. The final snapshot of miR-144 at 1 μs was used in this study. MiR-29a contains 22 nucleotides, including seven adenines (A), five cytosines (C), four guanines (G), and six uracils (U) (Fig. 1A). MiR-144 contains 22 nucleotides, including eight adenines (A), three cytosines (C), four guanines (G), and seven uracils (U) (Fig. 1B). Two systems were set in this study: (I) A miR-29a/miR-144 mixture (named “miR”) where four miR-29a molecules and four miR-144 molecules were placed randomly (Each miRNA was placed at least 0.1 nm away from each other) (Fig. 1C) and (II) A mixture of 4 miR-29a chains and 4 miR-144 chains in a GQD solution. In a mixture, each miRNA was placed randomly at least 0.1 nm around GQD (named “miR_GQD”) (Fig. 1D). A GQD sheet has a dimension of 4.8 × 4.2 nm2 constructed by a Nanotube Builder module implemented in the Visual Molecular Dynamics (VMD) program. 65. A GQD topology was generated using OPLS forcefields 66. Four miR-29a molecules were labelled as chain A to D, whereas chain E to H stand for four miR-144 molecules (Fig. 1C and D). Both systems were placed in a simulation box (cubic size: 16 x 16 × 16 nm3) and solvated by TIP3P waters under 1M NaCl condition. For each system, 3000 steps of energy minimisation were run to remove bad contacts with the steepest descent algorithm (The details of the condition used are presented in “MD Simulation Protocols” section). The equilibration run was performed for 50 ns followed by the 1 μs production run.

GROMACS v.2020 package (www.gromacs.org) [38] was used to carry out all MD simulations with AMBER99SB-ILDN force field [39]. A harmonic potential with a spring constant of 1000 kJ mol−1 nm−2 was applied to maintain stability within the system. Long-range electrostatic interactions were calculated using the particle mesh Ewald (PME) method [40,41], with a short-range cutoff of 1 nm, a Fourier spacing of 0.12 nm, and fourth-order spline interpolation. The LINCS method [42] was used to constrain bond lengths within each system. To simulate an infinite system, periodic boundary conditions were applied in all directions. All simulations were performed under constant particle number, pressure, and temperature (NPT) conditions. The v-rescale thermostat [43] was used to maintain the temperature of the miRNA, graphene quantum dots (GQDs as named “GRA” in this study), and solvent ions individually at 300 K with a coupling constant τ t = 0.1 ps. The pressure was coupled using Parrinello-Rahman algorithm at 1 bar with a coupling constant τ p = 1 ps. The time step for integration was set to 2 fs, and coordinates were saved every 2 ps.

The data generated from the MD simulations were analyzed using a combination of GROMACS tools and locally written code. Graphical figures were generated by VMD [44]. The RMSD was computed using an initial structure from each production as a reference. The hydrogen bonds were performed using gmx hbond with default parameters, which included a hydrogen-donor-acceptor cutoff angle is 30° and a cutoff radius (X-acceptor) of 0.35 nm. The percentage of contacts was calculated with a cutoff value of 0.35 nm.

To determine the structural properties of miRNAs, the structural flexibilities of each miRNA are investigated via Root-Mean-Square Deviations (RMSDs) (Fig. 2 ). Both types of miRNAs appear to be mobile in a solution as seen by fluctuated RMSDs. The drift of RMSDs is observed when miRNAs start to aggregate in both systems (see the insets in Fig. 2). Before the assembly, miRNAs in all systems are very mobile (before 200 ns in miR and 100 ns in miR_GQD), but more rigid miRNAs are observed when they start to associate (Fig. 2A and B). It can be seen in Fig. 2C that the miRNA aggregation and formation of miRNA-GQD complex can be formed within 250 ns. For miR system, no complete miRNA aggregation is found. This is due to the repulsive forces between negative charges of each miRNA. Nonetheless, some miRNAs can still assemble. It is shown in Fig. 2C that miR-29a can bind both miR-144 and miR-29a using its 5’ end. More details will be explained later in the text.

For miR_GQD, GQD can collect miRNAs which agrees well with our previous studies [8,31,45,46]. The curvature of GQD is also found in this work (Fig. S1 in supplementary information). This curvy GQD structure can also be seen in other studies [8,47,48]. Overall, four miRNAs are coated on a GQD surface where GQD seems to be miR-29a-selective. This is because three miR-29a are adsorbed on GQD, while only one miR-144 is found on GQD (Fig. 2B and C). This observed selectivity is due to the difference in secondary structures between miR-29a and miR-144. MiR-144 shows circular-like structure, whereas miR-29a has a hairpin loop at 3′ tail and free 5′ end (Fig. 1A). Such free 5’ terminus of miR-29a is thus flexible enough to stick on GQD. The attachment by stem-ending tail was also seen in previous computational and experimental studies [49,50]. Although GQD shows promising ability to collect short miRNA, it is noticeable that GQD prefers non-circular miRNA with a free end. This is because the free terminus seems to easily grab GQD. More interaction details will be discussed later in the text.

To examine the effect of aggregation on miRNA structure, the self-hydrogen bonds of each miRNA are computed in Table 1 . Comparing with free single miRNA in a bulk, both types of miRNAs seem to maintain their characteristics. No significant change in a number of self-interactions is found (Table 1). This implies all miRNAs are not unfolded. MiR-29a employs hydrogen bonds of 22.97
±
3.21 to keep its structure in a bulk, while slightly higher number of hydrogen bonds are found in miR (∼22–25 hydrogen bonds) and miR_GQD (∼22–26 hydrogen bonds) (Fig. 3 A). A high number of self-hydrogen bonds (∼26 bonds) in chain D (blue miR-29a in Fig. 2C) are found because it is tightly packed and agglomerated onto a GQD surface. The adsorption of miR-29a molecules on GQD has a miniature impact on their internal hydrogen bonds. For miR-144, it forms 21.49
±
5.83 hydrogen bonds in water (Fig. 3A). A presence of other miRNAs and GQD does not unfold miR-144 in all cases (∼23–25 hydrogen bonds in miR and ∼22–25 bonds in miR_GQD). This implies the morphology of such miRNA molecules is well-preserved.

In addition, a number of miRNA contacts with GQD and water are also measured in Table 2 . Approximately, both types of miRNAs can form ∼3100–3300 contacts with water molecules. A presence of GQD seems to have minor impact on their folding due to a comparable number of water contacts and internal interactions of all miRNAs in miR_GQD (Table 2). For example, chains A, B, D, and F are coated on GQD surface in miR_GQD, but their number of water contacts and self-hydrogen bonds are comparable to miR system. Previously, DNA/RNA molecules were reported to be seriously denatured during the adsorption on micro-sized nanomaterials including graphene [36,51–54]. Interestingly, nano-sized graphene (GQD) here does not unfold miRNAs. This finding implies the ability of nano-sized graphene quantum dot to preserve the miRNA structure which allows miRNA to be more exposure to probes or other agents for further isolation and detection. For GQD-bound miRNAs (three miR-29a (chain A, B, and D) and one miR-144 (chain F)), two miRNA conformations on GQD are identified. The “laying down” and “standing upright” poses are captured (Fig. S1B in supplementary information). These two conformations can also be experimentally and theoretically seen in other RNA/DNA studies [8,49,50]. Chain A (miR-29a) aligns most part on GQD resulting in the highest number of GQD contacts (∼298 contacts), while chain F (miR-144) stands uptight leading to the least GQD contacts (∼105.5 contacts) (Table 2). For the rest, chain B and D employ their 5′ end and the tip of hairpin loop to interact with GQD where such poses require 126–133 contacts (Table 2). It appears that the free 5’ end seems to induce more GQD contacts. A circular miR-144 contains no free end so this leads to the difficulty for nucleobases to interact with GQD surface.

Furthermore, to explore the clustering mechanism, a number of inter-hydrogen bonds between chains are also computed (Fig. 4 ). It can be seen in Fig. 4 that miR-29a and miR-144 can assemble although only transient aggregation is captured (Figs. 2C and 4). Interestingly, the miRNA-GQD pairing is permanent (Fig. 2C). This finding can highlight the potential use of GQD as a miRNA collector. In miR, both types of miRNAs can interact with each other, but the highest number of hydrogen bonds between a miR-29a/miR-29a pair (chain B and D) suggests more compact structure than the miR-29a/miR-144 and miR-144/miR-144 clustering (Fig. 4A). Such high number of miR-29a/miR-29a hydrogen bonds (∼8 hydrogen bonds in Fig. 4A) are because both miR-29a employ their 5′ ends to pair up. In case of miR-144, it can also interact with other miRNAs by breaking down the self-interactions between their stem-ending regions. After breaking non-covalent bond between 5′ and 3′ ends, miR-144 can either twist its nucleobases out to the bulk to allow the formation of inter-hydrogen bonds or employ its 3′ terminus to interact with others (Fig. 4A). Seemingly, our findings demonstrate the miRNA aggregation ability is secondary structure-dependent. The hairpin-like structure with a free tail displays the good capability to be assembled than a circular-like structure. The presence of the free and flexible tail is also beneficial for GQD adsorption. As reported earlier, GQD prefers miR-29a to miR-144 due to the free 5′ end. Such terminus triggers the fast and spontaneous miR-29a/GQD assembly. In miR_GQD, chain A and D on GQD can also interact with each other using their 5′ ends (Fig. 4B). Apart from GQD-bound miRNAs, the rest (one miR-29a (chain C) and three miR-144 (chain E, G, and H)) are found to float in a solution and pair with another miRNA (Fig. 4B). As expected, hairpin-like chain C (miR-29a) forms high number of hydrogen bonds with chain H (miR-144) using its 5’ tail (Fig. 4B). Besides, it is found that chain E and G which are miR-144 can also pair up together in the bulk using the top of their hairpins (Fig. 4B).

To further investigate how each miRNA orients on a GQD surface, the frequencies of GQD contacts are computed in Fig. 5 . Like miR-29a in previous studies [8,46], miR-29a here mainly employs its free and flexible 5′ tail to adhere on a GQD surface, whereas the tip of its hairpin loop (residues 14–17) can also interact with GQD (Fig. 5A and C). Chain A is found to bind tightly to GQD because almost all parts are adhered on a GQD surface. Only a hairpin region (except the tip) is found to be GQD-unbound (Fig. 5A). Other GQD-bound miR-29a molecules also utilize the 5’ tail and the tip of hairpin structure to sit on GQD (Fig. 5A and C). Using stem-ending zones of DNA/RNA to adhere on graphene were also previously reported in both experimental and computational studies [49,50]. In case of miR-144 (chain F), only a tip of hairpin structure (residue 14–19) is found to interact with GQD (Fig. 5B and C). Our results reveal the importance of miRNA morphology on miRNA/GQD clustering. A presence of the free end seems to facilitate the effective binding to GQD.

Herein, the aggregation mechanisms of miRNA mixture with and without GQD has been studied. A mixture of miR-29a (hairpin-like structure) and miR-144 (circular-like structure) molecules is used in this work. GQD acts as an excellent miRNA sorbent. Previously, both DNA and RNA were reported to be completely denatured during the adsorption on a micro-sized graphene [36,51–54]. Herein, nano-sized graphene or graphene quantum dot seems to preserve miRNA scaffolds. This phenomenon is beneficial for further miRNA desorption because such preserved structure allows the easy accessibility of water and probes. The desorption mechanism will be our future work to prove this hypothesis. In addition, the GQD/miRNA clustering depends on the miRNA morphology. GQD appears to be miR-29a-selecitve. This is because the presence of hairpin organisation with a free terminus allows miR-29a to be easily GQD-accessible. Unlike miR-29a, although circular-like miR-144 can adhere on GQD surface, it requires the adjustment of its structure for GQD-miRNA aggregation. This insight will be useful for designing selective GQD-based miRNA isolation applications.

Darunee Traiphothon: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tadsanee Awang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Investigation. Nattapon Kuntip: Visualization, Investigation. Deanpen Japrung: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Prapasiri Pongprayoon: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: This manuscript is original, has not been published before and is not currently being considered for publication elsewhere. There are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Kasetsart University Research and Development Institute (Grant no. FF(KU)51.67), National Nanotechnology Center (NANOTEC, Grant no. P2351465 and P2250764) and the Office of the National Economic and Social Development Council, the Office of the Prime Minister through Kasetsart University under the project entitled "Driving Research and Development of Cutting-edge Innovations for ASEAN's Agricultural Leadership" for financial support for financial supports. We are also thankful for the computer facilities from Department of Computer Engineering, Kasetsart University and NSTDA supercomputer center (ThaiSC).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jmgm.2024.108881.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MiR-29a promotes cell proliferation and EMT in breast cancer by targeting ten eleven translocation 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparison of umbrella sampling and steered molecular dynamics methods for computing free energy profiles of aromatic substrates through phospholipid bilayers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Noh, S.Y., Notman, R. Comparison of umbrella sampling and steered molecular dynamics methods for computing free energy profiles of aromatic substrates through phospholipid bilayers. The Journal of Chemical Physics. 2020, 153, 034115.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: MicroRNA biomarkers for infectious diseases: from basic Research to biosensing
